items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: Why is the electric field between parallel charged plates considered uniform?
items.correct: Field strength is constant in magnitude and direction throughout the region (away from edges), unlike the inverse-square field of a point charge.
items.misconception: The field still decreases with the inverse square of distance — uniform just means it's smooth.
items.random_wrong: Because the plates are uniform in shape.
items.why: Students extrapolate inverse-square thinking everywhere; they don't distinguish point sources from extended geometries.

items.q: Sound is what type of wave?
items.why: Correct answer: Longitudinal. “Transverse” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: We see lightning before hearing thunder because.
items.correct: Light is much faster than sound
items.why: It's Light is much faster than sound. “Lightning is louder” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A wave has frequency 5 Hz and wavelength 4 m. What is its speed in m/s? (speed = frequency x wavelength)
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
